ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Kadai Subzi; 'vegetable curry (food product)' is the closest defensible match. Exact Kadai Subzi entry is absent from FoodOn as of April 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (Vegetable dishes) is the closest defensible category. No specific Kadai Subzi code exists in FoodEx2 as of April 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Kadai Subzi is approximately 250 g, typically served as a side dish with roti, naan, or rice in a North Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mixed Vegetable Curry (Sabzi)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) reports values for mixed vegetable curry (sabzi) cooked with oil and spices. Kadai Subzi is a variant of this preparation cooked in a wok (kadai) with capsicum, onion, tomato, and a kadai masala spice blend. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry entry; sodium is adjusted upward to reflect restaurant-level seasoning typical of Singapore North Indian restaurant servings. The spicy variant does not materially alter macronutrient composition relative to the standard mixed vegetable curry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed vegetable curry / non-starchy vegetable dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kadai Subzi. GI of 45 is estimated by analogy to mixed non-starchy vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 international GI tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021). Non-starchy vegetable curries with oil and spice bases consistently fall in the GI range 40–55. A GI of 45 (low category) is the central defensible estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 17.0) / 100 = 7.65, rounded to 7.7. Serving carbohydrate = 6.8 g per 100 g × 250 g serving = 17.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from restaurant-level seasoning and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g restaurant serving of Kadai Subzi contributes approximately 800 mg sodium, representing about 35% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by major cardiovascular guidelines; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and high dietary fibre from non-starchy vegetables
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kadai Subzi has a low estimated GI of approximately 45 and provides 2.1 g dietary fibre per 100 g, making it a suitable vegetable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with a controlled carbohydrate staple such as a small portion of roti or brown rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, cholesterol-free, and rich in antioxidant spices and vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kadai Subzi is low in saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g), contains no cholesterol, and is rich in capsicum and tomato providing vitamin C and lycopene; however, the moderate sodium level from restaurant preparation warrants attention for individuals managing cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kadai Subzi (also spelled Kadai Sabzi or Karahi Sabzi) is a North Indian spiced mixed vegetable dish cooked in a kadai, a traditional Indian wok with steep sides. The dish features a colourful combination of mixed vegetables — typically including capsicum (bell pepper), onion, tomato, cauliflower, carrot, and green peas — stir-fried and simmered in a robust tomato-onion gravy seasoned with a kadai masala spice blend comprising cumin, coriander seeds, dried red chilli, garam masala, ginger, and garlic. The cooking technique in the kadai imparts a characteristic smoky, slightly charred flavour and ensures the vegetables retain some texture rather than becoming fully soft. The spicy variant uses a higher proportion of red chilli and black pepper, producing a pungent, deeply aromatic dish with moderate to high heat. Kadai Subzi is entirely plant-based, making it suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets, and is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish at approximately 72 kcal per 100 g, with a favourable macronutrient profile: low in saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g), moderate in dietary fibre (2.1 g per 100 g), and providing useful amounts of vitamin C (18 mg per 100 g) and vitamin A (55 mcg RAE per 100 g) from the capsicum and tomato base. The estimated glycemic index is low (~45), making it appropriate for blood glucose management. In Singapore, Kadai Subzi is served at North Indian restaurants as a main vegetable side dish, typically accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice, and is priced in the SGD 16–17 range at establishments such as Mirch Masalah, Khushboo, and Curry Village.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kadai subzi?
What vegetables are in kadai subzi?
Is kadai subzi good for diabetes?
What is the GI of kadai sabzi?
meal_context: side dish with roti or naan

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) mixed vegetable curry entry, which is the closest approved primary source for this dish. No exact Kadai Subzi entry exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy vegetable curry. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry (sabzi) data; exact Kadai Subzi values are not available in any approved primary source as of April 2026. 2. Sodium (320 mg per 100 g) is adjusted upward from the ICMR-NIN baseline to reflect typical restaurant-level seasoning observed in Singapore North Indian restaurants. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Kadai Subzi was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): ICMR-NIN reports no trans fat for vegetable oil-based vegetable curry preparations. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is an entirely plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero. 8. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally absent. 9. The spicy variant does not materially alter macronutrient composition relative to standard mixed vegetable curry. 10. Dietary flags assume standard restaurant preparation without dairy (no cream or ghee); preparations with added cream or ghee would alter the dairy-free and vegan flags.
